APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): This paper addresses a core NLP problem (word- and document-level sentiment) using distributional semantics and is therefore squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML conferences. The methodological framing (Word2Vec-style vectors, PCA-based reference construction) and datasets used (IMDB, SST) are appropriate, but the evaluation practices raise concerns for a top venue: only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, key evaluation metrics are not reported, and reproducibility details (code availability) are not mentioned. With stronger, standard baselines, clearer metrics, and full reproducibility materials the work would be an excellent fit. | B(4): This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML conference scope: it tackles sentiment orientation using distributional semantics and evaluates on standard datasets (IMDB, SST). The formulation—unsupervised embedding-based SO via reference vectors—and the comparison to a classic baseline (PMI-IR) are appropriate for the venue and target audience of researchers in sentiment analysis and semantic representation. My only reservation is modest: while the approach and experiments (word2vec, PCA, IMDB/SST) meet community expectations, the summary does not indicate broader baselines or detailed evaluation metrics, which could limit perceived contribution at a top-tier venue.

reviewer_a.justification: This paper addresses a core NLP problem (word- and document-level sentiment) using distributional semantics and is therefore squarely within the scope and audience of top NLP/ML conferences. The methodological framing (Word2Vec-style vectors, PCA-based reference construction) and datasets used (IMDB, SST) are appropriate, but the evaluation practices raise concerns for a top venue: only PMI-IR is used as a baseline, key evaluation metrics are not reported, and reproducibility details (code availability) are not mentioned. With stronger, standard baselines, clearer metrics, and full reproducibility materials the work would be an excellent fit.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper sits squarely within NLP/ML conference scope: it tackles sentiment orientation using distributional semantics and evaluates on standard datasets (IMDB, SST). The formulation—unsupervised embedding-based SO via reference vectors—and the comparison to a classic baseline (PMI-IR) are appropriate for the venue and target audience of researchers in sentiment analysis and semantic representation. My only reservation is modest: while the approach and experiments (word2vec, PCA, IMDB/SST) meet community expectations, the summary does not indicate broader baselines or detailed evaluation metrics, which could limit perceived contribution at a top-tier venue.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a plausible embedding-based method and compares against PMI-IR on IMDB and SST, but the experimental description (evaluation metrics, statistical significance testing, and precise dataset splits/selection) is incomplete in the summary, limiting confidence in the claimed superiority. There is some discussion of PMI-IR’s limitations, and hyperparameters are reported, yet key reproducibility elements (random seeds, preprocessing/tokenization, code release) and targeted ablations (e.g., effect of K, PCA dimensionality, choice of seed words, embedding training regime) appear missing. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but failure cases and sensitivity analyses are insufficiently explored to fully support robustness claims. | B(3): The paper evaluates the proposed Word2Vec/PCA-based sentiment orientation method on two standard datasets (IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and directly compares to Turney’s PMI-IR baseline, which supports the main empirical claim. However, key experimental details are missing or limited: evaluation metrics are not specified, ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of PCA dimensionality, seed choice, vector training hyperparameters or corpus size) are not reported, and failure modes or limits of the new method are not thoroughly discussed beyond critiquing PMI-IR. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are given, which aids reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some experimental choices lack sufficient analysis, so the evidence is adequate but missing important robustness experiments.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a plausible embedding-based method and compares against PMI-IR on IMDB and SST, but the experimental description (evaluation metrics, statistical significance testing, and precise dataset splits/selection) is incomplete in the summary, limiting confidence in the claimed superiority. There is some discussion of PMI-IR’s limitations, and hyperparameters are reported, yet key reproducibility elements (random seeds, preprocessing/tokenization, code release) and targeted ablations (e.g., effect of K, PCA dimensionality, choice of seed words, embedding training regime) appear missing. Related work coverage is described as thorough, but failure cases and sensitivity analyses are insufficiently explored to fully support robustness claims.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates the proposed Word2Vec/PCA-based sentiment orientation method on two standard datasets (IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank) and directly compares to Turney’s PMI-IR baseline, which supports the main empirical claim. However, key experimental details are missing or limited: evaluation metrics are not specified, ablation/sensitivity analyses (e.g., effect of PCA dimensionality, seed choice, vector training hyperparameters or corpus size) are not reported, and failure modes or limits of the new method are not thoroughly discussed beyond critiquing PMI-IR. Related work coverage is reported as thorough and hyperparameters are given, which aids reproducibility, but code is not mentioned and some experimental choices lack sufficient analysis, so the evidence is adequate but missing important robustness experiments.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (novel Word2Vec-based sentiment orientation, robustness to sparsity, full-vocabulary scoring). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are generally informative; writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary, code availability is not mentioned, and some methodological steps (e.g., exact review labeling procedure and PCA/component interpretation) could be made more explicit to aid reproducibility. | B(4): The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel Word2Vec-based sentiment orientation and comparison to PMI-IR). Notation and methodological steps (K×K cosine matrix, PCA, reference-vector construction, SO definition) are presented coherently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective. However, some presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and a few methodological choices (e.g., dimensionality reduction details and exact review-labeling procedure) could be explained more explicitly to remove residual ambiguity.

reviewer_a.justification: Overall the paper is well organized and follows a logical flow from introduction through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (novel Word2Vec-based sentiment orientation, robustness to sparsity, full-vocabulary scoring). Notation appears consistent and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are generally informative; writing quality is good. Minor presentation issues reduce clarity: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary, code availability is not mentioned, and some methodological steps (e.g., exact review labeling procedure and PCA/component interpretation) could be made more explicit to aid reproducibility.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized with a clear flow from introduction through method, experiments, and conclusion, and the main contributions are stated early (novel Word2Vec-based sentiment orientation and comparison to PMI-IR). Notation and methodological steps (K×K cosine matrix, PCA, reference-vector construction, SO definition) are presented coherently and hyperparameters are reported, and figures/tables/examples are described as effective. However, some presentation gaps remain: evaluation metrics are not specified in the summary and a few methodological choices (e.g., dimensionality reduction details and exact review-labeling procedure) could be explained more explicitly to remove residual ambiguity.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper tackles a useful problem—unsupervised word-level sentiment orientation—and demonstrates a practical alternative to PMI-IR that is more robust to data sparsity and works on smaller corpora (evaluated on IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank). Its approach (Word2Vec embeddings, PCA-based axis, positive/negative reference vectors) and the claim of outperforming PMI-IR make it a solid, usable contribution for practitioners who need lexicon induction from limited data. However, the idea is an incremental application of distributional methods rather than a paradigm shift, so it is likely to have moderate impact within sentiment-analysis and embedding-based lexicon induction rather than broadly transforming the field. | B(3): The paper tackles an important practical problem—unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation—and proposes a distributional-semantic (Word2Vec-based) method that reportedly outperforms the classic PMI-IR approach while requiring much smaller corpora and producing orientations for all vocabulary items. From my perspective, this is a useful, pragmatic advance that could influence practitioners who lack huge corpora and who want full-vocabulary sentiment scores (tested on IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank). However, the contribution appears incremental relative to the broader literature on embedding-based lexicon induction and its wider impact depends on comparisons to more recent baselines and broader evaluations; thus it is unlikely to be transformative but is a solid, moderately impactful contribution.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles a useful problem—unsupervised word-level sentiment orientation—and demonstrates a practical alternative to PMI-IR that is more robust to data sparsity and works on smaller corpora (evaluated on IMDB and Stanford Sentiment Treebank). Its approach (Word2Vec embeddings, PCA-based axis, positive/negative reference vectors) and the claim of outperforming PMI-IR make it a solid, usable contribution for practitioners who need lexicon induction from limited data. However, the idea is an incremental application of distributional methods rather than a paradigm shift, so it is likely to have moderate impact within sentiment-analysis and embedding-based lexicon induction rather than broadly transforming the field.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—unsupervised induction of word-level sentiment orientation—and proposes a distributional-semantic (Word2Vec-based) method that reportedly outperforms the classic PMI-IR approach while requiring much smaller corpora and producing orientations for all vocabulary items. From my perspective, this is a useful, pragmatic advance that could influence practitioners who lack huge corpora and who want full-vocabulary sentiment scores (tested on IMDB and the Stanford Sentiment Treebank). However, the contribution appears incremental relative to the broader literature on embedding-based lexicon induction and its wider impact depends on comparisons to more recent baselines and broader evaluations; thus it is unlikely to be transformative but is a solid, moderately impactful contribution.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper presents a reasonably novel application-level combination—using distributional word vectors plus PCA to derive a sentiment axis and compute orientation for all vocabulary words—which meaningfully differs from Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-based approach. However, the core components (word embeddings, cosine distances, and PCA/projection for semantic dimensions) are established techniques in distributional semantics and prior evaluations of unsupervised semantic-orientation methods exist, so the contribution reads as an incremental but useful methodological synthesis rather than a radical new idea. The authors do clearly differentiate from PMI-IR and emphasize advantages for data sparsity and vocabulary coverage, which would be informative to experts but not deeply surprising.  | B(3): The paper combines known components (word embeddings, cosine distances and PCA) into a pipeline to induce a sentiment axis and compute orientation for all vocabulary words, which is a practical and useful extension beyond Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-oriented method. However, the high-level idea—finding a sentiment direction in distributional space and projecting words onto it—is an incremental step rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, since it mainly swaps PMI queries for embedding-based distances and uses PCA to extract an axis. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from Turney (better coverage, robustness to sparsity) and report novel design choices (reference-vector averaging and SO as cosine-difference), but these are non-trivial engineering refinements rather than a radical methodological advance, so the contribution rates as moderate novelty to a field expert.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper presents a reasonably novel application-level combination—using distributional word vectors plus PCA to derive a sentiment axis and compute orientation for all vocabulary words—which meaningfully differs from Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-based approach. However, the core components (word embeddings, cosine distances, and PCA/projection for semantic dimensions) are established techniques in distributional semantics and prior evaluations of unsupervised semantic-orientation methods exist, so the contribution reads as an incremental but useful methodological synthesis rather than a radical new idea. The authors do clearly differentiate from PMI-IR and emphasize advantages for data sparsity and vocabulary coverage, which would be informative to experts but not deeply surprising.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper combines known components (word embeddings, cosine distances and PCA) into a pipeline to induce a sentiment axis and compute orientation for all vocabulary words, which is a practical and useful extension beyond Turney’s PMI-IR phrase-oriented method. However, the high-level idea—finding a sentiment direction in distributional space and projecting words onto it—is an incremental step rather than a fundamentally new paradigm, since it mainly swaps PMI queries for embedding-based distances and uses PCA to extract an axis. The authors do clearly differentiate their approach from Turney (better coverage, robustness to sparsity) and report novel design choices (reference-vector averaging and SO as cosine-difference), but these are non-trivial engineering refinements rather than a radical methodological advance, so the contribution rates as moderate novelty to a field expert.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper states clear main claims and provides an experimental comparison to PMI-IR using IMDB and Stanford Sentiment data, and reports hyperparameters — supporting reproducibility to some extent. However, important methodological details are missing in the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, statistical significance testing and code release are not mentioned, and only a single compared baseline (PMI-IR) is used, which weakens the evidence that the method generally outperforms alternatives. The PCA-driven dimensionality reduction and seed-selection steps introduce assumptions that lack ablation or sensitivity analyses in the provided summary, so while the approach is promising the empirical support is incomplete. | B(3): The paper states clear claims and presents an embedding+PCA method applied to IMDB and SST, and reports outperforming PMI‑IR, but the experimental evidence as summarized is incomplete (evaluation metrics not reported, statistical testing absent, and only a single baseline used). There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the method is simple and plausible, but key methodological choices (reducing to 2D via PCA, seeding/averaging procedure, treatment of vocabulary coverage) are not theoretically justified and could introduce artifacts. The comparison to PMI‑IR is valid but weak as a sole baseline given modern alternatives; thus the conclusions are tentatively supported but suffer from missing experimental rigor and some unsupported assumptions.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear main claims and provides an experimental comparison to PMI-IR using IMDB and Stanford Sentiment data, and reports hyperparameters — supporting reproducibility to some extent. However, important methodological details are missing in the summary: evaluation metrics are not specified, statistical significance testing and code release are not mentioned, and only a single compared baseline (PMI-IR) is used, which weakens the evidence that the method generally outperforms alternatives. The PCA-driven dimensionality reduction and seed-selection steps introduce assumptions that lack ablation or sensitivity analyses in the provided summary, so while the approach is promising the empirical support is incomplete.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper states clear claims and presents an embedding+PCA method applied to IMDB and SST, and reports outperforming PMI‑IR, but the experimental evidence as summarized is incomplete (evaluation metrics not reported, statistical testing absent, and only a single baseline used). There are no mathematical proofs to verify, and the method is simple and plausible, but key methodological choices (reducing to 2D via PCA, seeding/averaging procedure, treatment of vocabulary coverage) are not theoretically justified and could introduce artifacts. The comparison to PMI‑IR is valid but weak as a sole baseline given modern alternatives; thus the conclusions are tentatively supported but suffer from missing experimental rigor and some unsupported assumptions.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper compares primarily to the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and highlights PMI-IR's data-sparsity limitations, which is appropriate as a historical reference but insufficient as a contemporary evaluation. Comparisons appear partially fair insofar as both methods are tested on IMDB (and SST), but the paper does not report clear evaluation metrics or statistical testing and notes PMI-IR could only label a reduced test set, which undermines direct comparability. The authors acknowledge PMI-IR's weaknesses, and they describe their method's differences, but they omit important competing baselines (e.g., other unsupervised embedding-based sentiment measures, lexicon-based approaches, or more recent unsupervised/supervised baselines) and do not present ablations or significance analyses to support claimed gains. Overall the experimental comparisons are incomplete and leave reproducibility/robustness questions unanswered. | B(3): The paper compares primarily to the classic PMI-IR (Turney 2002) baseline, which is relevant but insufficient given more recent embedding- and distributional-based lexicon induction methods; this is a notable omission for a Word2Vec-based proposal. The experimental setup appears partly unfair: PMI-IR could only be evaluated on a restricted subset of reviews due to pattern requirements, which inflates the apparent advantage of the new method despite using the same datasets. The authors do discuss PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and provide differences in method, but they do not benchmark against contemporary unsupervised/embedding baselines or thoroughly analyze when prior methods might match or exceed their performance.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares primarily to the classic PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) baseline and highlights PMI-IR's data-sparsity limitations, which is appropriate as a historical reference but insufficient as a contemporary evaluation. Comparisons appear partially fair insofar as both methods are tested on IMDB (and SST), but the paper does not report clear evaluation metrics or statistical testing and notes PMI-IR could only label a reduced test set, which undermines direct comparability. The authors acknowledge PMI-IR's weaknesses, and they describe their method's differences, but they omit important competing baselines (e.g., other unsupervised embedding-based sentiment measures, lexicon-based approaches, or more recent unsupervised/supervised baselines) and do not present ablations or significance analyses to support claimed gains. Overall the experimental comparisons are incomplete and leave reproducibility/robustness questions unanswered.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares primarily to the classic PMI-IR (Turney 2002) baseline, which is relevant but insufficient given more recent embedding- and distributional-based lexicon induction methods; this is a notable omission for a Word2Vec-based proposal. The experimental setup appears partly unfair: PMI-IR could only be evaluated on a restricted subset of reviews due to pattern requirements, which inflates the apparent advantage of the new method despite using the same datasets. The authors do discuss PMI-IR’s data-sparsity limitations and provide differences in method, but they do not benchmark against contemporary unsupervised/embedding baselines or thoroughly analyze when prior methods might match or exceed their performance.

main_claims: Introduces a novel distributional-semantic (Word2Vec-based) approach to measure word-level sentiment orientation using multi-dimensional vector spaces.
The proposed unsupervised method outperforms the existing unsupervised PMI-IR approach (Turney, 2002) while requiring much smaller datasets and being more robust to data sparsity.
The method computes sentiment orientation for all vocabulary words (not just phrases) by constructing positive/negative reference vectors and measuring cosine-distance differences.
_summary.methodology: Build high-dimensional word vectors from the IMDB corpus (using word-embedding methods), select point words (adjectives/adverbs), compute a K×K cosine-distance matrix, apply PCA to reduce to 2 dimensions, form positive/negative reference vectors by averaging words on either side of the principal axis (seeded by “excellent”), and define sentiment orientation SO(w) as CosDst(Vec_pos, w) − CosDst(Vec_neg, w). Reviews are labeled by the mean SO across contained words/phrases.
limitations: PMI-IR (Turney, 2002) is constrained by corpus size and suffers from data sparsity when appropriate large corpora are not available.
PMI-IR is only applicable to reviews that contain at least one of the defined patterns, reducing the test set (they report 7,646 posts out of 9,613).

reviewer_a.name: Reproducible Methods Specialist
reviewer_a.background: Senior ML engineer with 10+ years building and evaluating NLP systems in both industry and academia; experience tuning embedding models and designing rigorous experiments for word-level and document-level tasks.
traits: looks for complete experimental details (hyperparameters, data preprocessing, random seeds) to allow replication
requires strong empirical comparisons (well-chosen baselines, ablation studies, and statistical significance testing)
values clarity on dataset size/selection and sensitivity to data sparsity and hyperparameters
notices potential implementation pitfalls (embedding training regimen, tokenization, corpus leakage) and requests error analyses
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Focuses on methodological rigor and reproducibility: verifies that experiments are well-controlled, baselines are fair, and claims are supported by statistically robust results and ablations.

reviewer_b.name: Sentiment & Semantics Impact Researcher
reviewer_b.background: Associate professor specializing in sentiment analysis and distributional semantics, with a track record of work on lexicon induction, unsupervised sentiment orientation, and evaluation of semantic representations.
traits: evaluates novelty relative to classic and recent work (Turney 2002, later PMI-based and embedding-based lexicon induction)
assesses practical significance and applicability (vocabulary coverage, domain transfer, computational cost)
values insight into why a proposed method works (theoretical or empirical justification) and its limitations
interested in broader impacts, e.g., whether the method meaningfully advances sentiment analysis beyond existing tools
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on positioning, novelty, and real-world impact: judges if the contribution fills an important gap in sentiment lexicon induction and whether the paper advances understanding or practice in the field.
